text: Cool. Thank you. Thanks so much for coming. Hi. I'm Mary. I'm here all the way from
Orangery land, so-called Melbourne, Australia. I'm one of the creators of Roomy. That's an
at-proto-based group chat app for communities. I'm a guest here on this beautiful land which
is unceded ancestral territory of Musqueam people. I want to extend my respect to them and to all
indigenous people around the world who are protecting life from life and communal cultures
on this planet against colonial destruction. I guess if I had to sum up what I really want to say,
it's that communities are critically important and poorly understood and most of our tech is very
individual-oriented and that understanding how communities work, especially the role of organisers
and in particular when that community is about cooperating and coordinating to achieve something.
If we can understand that, we can better understand how social systems change more broadly.
And with that, I also just want to thank the organisers of this conference
for bringing this event together. It's a massive job.
I also personally really need to acknowledge this book which has just like had a really enormous impact
on my thinking. It draws on a lot of different thinkers on the topic of civilizational transition
away from our capitalist, colonial, developmentalist, extractive society and crisis.
It draws on critical thought and practice across disciplines and particularly from indigenous
and other communal cultures in the global south and especially in Latin America with the goal of
articulating an approach for design. And by design, I mean if we need a civilizational transition,
what are we transitioning to? Who gets to decide? If our current civilisation is one that systematically
destroys communal cultures, how can we design one with the conditions for communal life to flourish
and where cultures can practice the design of themselves? That's my North Star.
Roomie is what we've been working on. It's a tool for communities to chat, stay connected, work on things
together. When I first got involved, I wasn't necessarily thinking that another chat app was what
communities really need right now. But one of my co-founders, Ellen, had written up this idea that I found
compelling, which is that group chat is minimum viable community. Or in other words, chat can be the basic
foundation for communities to connect online. There are so many things that we could and should be building,
but chat is where we start. Anyone with internet access is familiar at some level with
the UX metaphor of the UX metaphor of a chat room to mean a specific context for communication between
specific people. You can absolutely do community in a chat room.
My partner and I had a baby in 2021 and at the time we weren't, we weren't close with any other queer
parents. And before I go on, I couldn't be here without my partner holding the floor while I'm away. So,
thank you, Asia and all the carers who make our connection here possible.
Becoming a parent can be isolating in a similar way to how coming out as queer can be isolating.
And it was scary. But people put us in touch with other friends in the community having kids and
we started a signal group chat that has honestly been a lifeline sometimes.
We try to practice mutual aid and show up for each other.
We organize to bring meals when someone just gave birth or is unwell.
We share advice and emotional support.
It's just a group chat. But that shared context lets us do community.
It's simple, but it's precious.
What happens when the needs of communities get more complex?
My drawings are a bit messed up.
Six years ago, my friends got me interested in a project.
They wanted to make a website with a calendar for activists and community organizers.
We'll all just keep it updated each week and with whatever we're seeing out in the world.
And I didn't really know how to make web apps at the time.
But somehow that didn't stop me from saying, what if we made a web app?
What if we gave organizers the tools to publish what they're doing?
And we created a map of all the different parts of our entire social movement.
And create new ways for people to connect with existing groups and for different groups
to work together and share resources all around the world.
I guess you could say that at the time, I didn't really understand the value of taking small steps.
We absolutely should have started by just manually updating a calendar.
Fast forward five years and I, by total chance, stumble across these guys, Ellen and Ziklag.

We had both independently spent the last few years trying to build community scale social apps
using matrix protocol.
Given up on that.
And started on a local first web publishing app.
We both had strong visions for what tech that puts users and communities first should look like.
And we had both learned the hard way, the importance of taking small steps.
We were both thinking about how collective resource sharing can defeat monopoly network effects.
We were both wondering what gets us in good loops to create good systems.

If you want to retroactively introduce some more structure and organization.
Roomie is not the first open source group chat app that has these features.
And there's still a lot of rough edges.
But the difference is that I -- the difference is that Roomie is an @proto app.
With this caveat, messages aren't on the PDS because we want to launch private spaces ASAP.
They live on our equivalent of a PDS called leaf.
It has a DID you can control to adversarially migrate it to your own self-hosted leaf server.
The data is formatted just like app proto records and you can build an alternative client too.
Auth is just an app proto service token.
All of which is to say we care about credible exit and interop.
And with permission spaces on the horizon, we're going to keep working to make interop as easy as possible.

Because users deserve easy access to their data on whatever app they want.

Less control to apps means users are more free and we can build cool stuff.
The whole point of social software is interconnecting.
AtProto lets us do that while meaningfully giving more control to users and, to some extent, communities.
At the start of this talk, I said, communities are critically important and poorly understood, but most of our tech is made with individuals in mind.
AtProto was designed for big world social networking, where individuals publish data for broadcasting across the whole network to be aggregated and consumed.
Just a name and shame.
But communities need to be somewhat separate, protected from the big world to really flourish.
Like I said before, the essence of a chat room is shared context.
When we can reliably establish shared context, shared norms, we can speak more freely, trust each other more easily, and get more stuff done together.
Okay, so, I mean, we live in an individualising, often isolating world.
And sometimes it feels like there is no alternative, nothing outside of that.
How do we enter the communal?
What happens in the inter-tidal zone between the big world and the protected space?
The late union organiser, Jane McAulvey, made some useful distinctions for the different levels of agency in collective action.
You can donate.
You can donate.
You could be advocating for an issue.
You can be mobilising people, and mobilising means you don't just want to inform people.
You want other people to get up and do something.
And when I think about how broadcast-based social media can help social movements, I think about mobilising.
You can mobilise a car, but what good is that if you don't know where you're going to drive it?
So, collectivity is made by organisers.
Organising is about empowering other people to have agency within the frame of your shared work or shared problem.
It's an evergreen meme.
Union organising is all about building collective people power.
Or fish power.
Each of us on our own might have pretty limited leverage in whatever problem we're facing.
But when a lot of us, with our limited leverage, choose to cooperate, we can collectively get big leverage.
That's the theory of change.
Organisers need tools that help communities create, refine, and communicate an effective mental model
of the system that they're trying to gain leverage in.
We want Rumi to be that tool, where it's not just about keeping the chat going,
but reflexively going back through the chat to strip out the noise and keep the key insights.
Putting messages into threads, letting threads become the seeds for refined documents, potentially even
publishing those documents for wider distribution.
Maybe sharing the key insights with other groups, other communities to coordinate and to find the others.
The public at Proto Social Graph can really help us with these points of interconnection.
So, the rest of this talk is like a vision board.
For what comes next.
One thing we want to build is a bit like web rings between communities.
Like, honestly, bring this back.
Web rings are a really cute practice back from the web 1.0 days.
People would form a network of personal sites by linking to each other in a ring.
So, you keep clicking next and you loop around back to the same site.
For us, I'm not sure if it should be in a ring, but we do really want to see how distinct communities
that are aligned in some way can interconnect without having to form a hierarchy.
So, I'm going to tell a story.
About a decade ago, I got involved in an environmental campaign called Stop Adani.
Adani is a mining company that was trying to build the biggest coal mine in the southern hemisphere.
And our government was funding them to do it.
I just hated the total inaction on climate change and needed to do something about it.
So, this ended up being my introduction to direct action, which is where you go and put your body on the line,
trying to stand directly in the way of something that a just society wouldn't tolerate.
When I got up to the camp, everyone was practicing consensus-based decision-making
with this idea that we don't pressure or coerce anyone to be part of an action.
We're working towards collective consent for whatever we do.
I started getting skilled up in facilitating meetings to learn how to make that happen
with a big group of people efficiently.
And being part of this practice really changed me.
I also learned that we had a few strategic goals.
But our main focus was to show investors that the project was unreliable.
To do that, we needed to identify choke points in their system and their process
and work out ways to exploit them and blow out their costs while minimizing our own.
I've participated to different degrees in a lot of actions and campaigns since then
with ups and downs.
But I think that campaign was where I really got a sense of how much is involved to organize
on that scale.
Ultimately, the mine did go ahead, but it was only a sixth of the scale that they'd proposed.
And the primary reason is they couldn't attract the capital.
So this is a review of the secondary targets to the campaign,
including financiers and how many of them publicly ruled out supporting the mine.
In the commons analysis in 2022, the mine had been delayed by nine years.
And the primary tactic they identified used by groups active in the campaign was information sharing.
So I came in to stop Adani in a relatively brief and limited capacity.
And I learned that what we were doing was just a small part of a much bigger system.
There were non-profits doing campaign research, strategic litigation.
There was volunteer community groups doing public campaigning.
There was a Wangan and Jagalingu traditional owners group opposing the mine.
We had links with groups based in student unions around the country.
And all of these were like different communities that were all coordinating with each other
and supporting each other with no hierarchy.
I only got a little sense of how much overhead went into that coordination.
But I'm going to say it was a lot of very thankless admin that is also critical organizing work.
But I hope with this example, you can imagine a future or a version of this reality where you could have
the Brisbane chapter of the non-profit, which is all volunteers, and the Sydney chapter,
and the funded national group, and a group for lawyers against Adani,
the traditional owners group, and the direct action camp,
probably being pretty quiet until we get end-to-end encryption.
And these are all autonomous community spaces accessed with Rumi or whatever client makes sense.
And none of them have to own each other, but they can express something about the relationship
with each other in their UI and their access control settings.
All of these groups want and need to be autonomous, and they also want and need to collaborate.
The current solutions force us to choose between the two.
So, to be clear, I'm not just trying to say hierarchy is bad.
Big communities often naturally have concentric rings of values and mission alignment, experience,
capacity to lead and act as an organizer, and that's why we see hierarchical role-based moderation,
for example, working pretty well for a lot of online communities.
It shouldn't necessarily be the default.
Yesterday, Blaine mentioned Nathan Schneider's concept of implicit feudalism,
and my thinking is that we can avoid that by creating tools that empower users to step into governance,
into organizing.
If people know Eleanor Ostrom, Governing the Commons, one of my key takeaways from that was
the ability to participate in modifying rules is one of the principles of successful commons governance.
In apps like Rumi, where a space can have many channels or other structures,
some of them might be private or have different access boundaries.
That structure can become complex in a way that's culturally integral.
We don't want that structure bound to any one app, so I think that's just like something to think about
as we move into permissioned spaces world.
How can we make governance structures interoperable?
Just to keep expanding the vision board, maybe there are some citizen scientists using a separate app
on Atproto where you can record observations of endangered species.
And at the same time, the lawyers against Adani are trying to mount an environmental
regulation case against the mine, and they have a feed set up where they can track
observations of the black-throated finch, the yakka skink, the ornamental snake,
within the specific region affected by the mine.
And they're all showing up automatically in their space as usable evidence.
The UI for rendering that data could be a plugin, someone published on Atproto,
maybe like a web tile or an inlay component, and the lawyers can choose how they want it rendered.
Soon there's a new space for organising citizen scientists in the area to
all travel out to the field together, specifically to gather observations to benefit the legal case.
So open social means the opportunities for people everywhere
to coordinate and get aligned on things we care about can just explode.
I wanted to talk about a climate-related campaign because the climate movement is full of
really smart people who have gotten good at system modelling.
Weather systems, sure. Ecologies, yes. But also working out how to coordinate and align incentives
between really diverse actors, states, market actors, non-profits, community groups around the whole world.
What we're doing with the open social web is really similar in a lot of ways.
Monopoly control of global communications infrastructure is also a planetary problem.
And we've been stuck in reinforcing feedback loops causing so much destruction.
Consider the effort with which fossil fuel companies have tried to prevent
increasingly cheap renewable energy from entering the market and cutting into their profits.
Like renewable energy, the benefits of everyone having control over their data
will be obvious to everyone once you break out of the monopoly feedback loop.
And there's no question that we will.
As I finish up, I just want to come back to this question from Designs for the Pluriverse.
What does it mean to design a civilisation in which communal life can flourish
and communities can practise the design of themselves?
The author argues that the concept of autonomy, like self-determination,
is a keystone in decolonial Latin American thought that could shape how we answer that question.
He proposes a framework called autonomous design.
I'd like to go into it more, but I'm going to just wrap up now.
But I'd love to talk about this with anyone.
This idea that communities are living systems, basically.
They produce themselves, and the more that they can reliably produce themselves
within the assurance of the holdfast, like Erin said, the better they can learn and adapt.
If we, as technologists, give communities tools to protect themselves against assault,
but also to learn and adapt to the broader environment,
we cultivate the conditions for a world where many worlds can fit.
